name: Computer Science Basics
description:
Fundamental concepts every programmer should know.

> **Note:** This quiz uses `question_limit: 3`, so each session randomly selects 3 out of 5 questions.

| Topic | Difficulty |
|---|---|
| Data Structures | Beginner |
| Loops | Beginner |
| Data Types | Beginner |
| Algorithms | Beginner |

questions.text: Which data structure follows the **FIFO** principle — First In, First Out?
questions.explanation:
A **Queue** follows FIFO — the first item added is the first one removed, like a line at a ticket booth.

```mermaid
graph LR
    In["➕ Enqueue"] --> A[Item 1]
    A --> B[Item 2]
    B --> C[Item 3]
    C --> Out["➖ Dequeue"]
```

A **Stack** follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) — like a stack of plates.

| Structure | Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Queue | FIFO | Printer queue, task scheduling |
| Stack | LIFO | Undo history, function call stack |

questions.text:
What is the time complexity of **accessing an element by index** in an array?

For example: `arr[5]`
questions.explanation:
Array access by index is $O(1)$ — **constant time** — because arrays store elements in contiguous memory. The computer can jump directly to the element using:

$$\text{address} = \text{base} + (\text{index} \times \text{element size})$$

No matter how large the array, accessing any index takes the same amount of time.

questions.text: Which type of loop is **guaranteed to run at least once**, regardless of the condition?
Which of the following are **primitive data types** found in most programming languages? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation:
Common primitive data types:

| Type | Example values | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `int` | `0`, `42`, `-7` | Whole numbers |
| `bool` | `true`, `false` | True/false values |
| `str` | `"hello"` | Text |
| `float` | `3.14`, `-0.5` | Decimal numbers |

A **function** is not a data type — it's a block of reusable code. (In some languages like Python, functions are *objects*, but they are not primitive types.)

questions.text: In your own words, describe what a **variable** is in programming and give a simple example.
questions.explanation:
A **variable** is a named storage location in memory that holds a value. You give it a name so you can use or change the value later.

Example in Python:
```
age = 25          # 'age' stores the integer 25
name = "Alice"    # 'name' stores a string
```

Variables can change over time:
```
score = 0
score = score + 10   # score is now 10
```

The name describes *what* is stored, making the code easier to read and understand.
